proposed_short: The stare + gerund construction (sto parlando, stavo leggendo) that frames an action as in progress at a given moment.
proposed_long: The progressive (forma progressiva, or presente/passato progressivo) presents an action as ongoing at a reference moment. Italian builds it with stare conjugated in the needed tense plus the invariable gerund: sto parlando (present, 'I am speaking'), stavo leggendo (past, 'I was reading'), starò lavorando (future, 'I will be working'). Only stare carries the person and tense; the gerund never changes. It is assembled with stare, never with essere ('sono parlando' is a beginner slip), and stare takes a gerund, never an infinitive ('sto parlare' is wrong). Unlike English, Italian uses the progressive sparingly: the plain present already covers most ongoing and habitual meaning, so 'mangio' often means 'I am eating'. The progressive is reserved for an action genuinely unfolding right now. (Choosing between the progressive and the plain present is a discrimination skill handled elsewhere; this entry describes the construction itself.)
rationale: Used throughout the gerundio progressive-assembly explanations and the progressive translation items. The glossary has 'gerund' (which mentions the stare construction in passing) but no entry for the progressive construction itself, which is the headline reason the gerund tree is built. Distinct from the gerund: the gerund is the -ando/-endo word; the progressive is the stare + gerund frame around it.

proposed_short: The gerundio passato: avendo or essendo plus a past participle (avendo finito, essendo partita), marking an action completed before the main clause.
proposed_long: The compound gerund (gerundio passato) expresses an action finished before the action of the main clause: 'Avendo finito i compiti, è uscito' (having finished his homework, he went out). It is built from the gerund of the auxiliary, avendo (avere) or essendo (essere), plus the past participle, mirroring the passato prossimo but with the auxiliary in its gerund form. The same auxiliary-choice and participle-agreement rules carry over: verbs that take avere keep the participle invariable (avendo mangiato), while verbs that take essere agree the participle with the subject (essendo andata, essendo partiti). It is a B1-B2 form, less frequent than the simple gerund but the natural way to mark anteriority in a gerund clause.
rationale: Used in the gerundio_passato grammar and translation explanations. The glossary has 'compound tense', 'passato prossimo' and 'trapassato prossimo' but no entry for this specific compound non-finite form; the 'gerundio passato' alias keeps it findable. Reuses 'auxiliary', 'past participle' and 'participle agreement', which already exist.
